topic: Someone receives an invitation to their high school reunion
raw_output:
[story 1]

The thick, cream-colored envelope felt heavy in Sarah’s hand, a physical weight mirroring the dull ache in her chest. A reunion invitation. It was from the alumni association, a formality she hadn't expected, and it landed like a stone in the already stagnant pool of her mood. She crumpled the paper once, the sound surprisingly loud in the quiet apartment. Looking at the embossed script—*Class of 2005*—a cold wave washed over her. She pictured the faces: polished, successful, laughing in sun-drenched settings. She felt a sharp tightening in her jaw, a need to simply burn the invitation, to erase the reminder of those years. She tossed it into the recycling bin with unnecessary force, the resulting rustle the only acknowledgment of the disappointment settling deep within her.

[story 2]

Mark stared at the invitation on his desk, the glossy paper mocking him with its cheerful typography. He didn't bother to open it immediately. Instead, he paced the length of his living room, his boots clicking a sharp rhythm on the hardwood floor. Every glance at the invitation felt like a spotlight illuminating every awkward moment, every perceived slight from his peers. He ran a hand through his hair, feeling the familiar, tight knot forming behind his eyes. "Why?" he muttered to the empty room, his voice raspy. "Why are they dragging me back into that circus?" He finally tore it open, the sound tearing through the silence, and immediately folded it into a tight, hard square, shoving it deep into a drawer as if sealing away a contagion.

[story 3]

When the mail arrived, David felt a profound sense of irritation bloom in his stomach. He picked up the invitation, his fingers brushing against the heavy cardstock, and his knuckles went white. He didn't read the details; he scanned the date, his eyes narrowing with undisguised disdain. The whole prospect felt like an imposition, a forced gathering of people whose achievements he felt were undeserved. He stood by the window, watching the street below, his posture rigid. A sour taste coated his tongue. He didn't move to discard it; instead, he placed it precisely on the edge of his bookshelf, a silent, deliberate placement signifying that this event was something to be acknowledged only by its existence, nothing more.
